input.instructions:
Solve this Super-Eywa grading-bank question.
Question ID: coding-B1-usaco-2024-us-open-platinum-identity-theft
Title: B1. USACO 2024 US Open Platinum / Identity Theft

Problem:
Extend cows' bitstring IDs so no cow can be mistaken for another when some reported IDs are truncated.

Grading target:
Binary hidden-test acceptance.

Return format:
FINAL_ANSWER: <your answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>

variables.selected_prompt_text:
Transform the provided problem into a rigorous, formal technical specification. Your goal is to strip away all narrative elements and provide a precise mathematical description of the input constraints, the required properties of the output set, and the optimization objective (e.g., lexicographical minimality or length minimality). Ensure the specification clearly defines the relationship between the input strings and the required output strings (such as the prefix-free constraint) so that a developer could implement a correct algorithmic solution without needing the original story context. Do not include specific names or example values from the problem.

Return exactly one JSON object in this format:
{
  "schema_name": "eywa_node_response",
  "schema_version": "v1",
  "orchestration_decision": "transmute",
  "decision_notes": "optional string",
  "message_for_next_agent": "plain-text reformulation for the next agent"
}
variables.child_selected_prompt_text:
Solve the question directly.
Keep the entire JSON compact.
Keep decision_notes extremely short. Prefer 3 to 8 words.
Do not narrate your reasoning outside the response field.
In the response field, use exactly two short lines encoded with \n:
FINAL_ANSWER: <answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>
Keep the justification to 12 words or fewer.
Use plain text only inside the response field. Do not use markdown formatting, code fences, or math-style backslashes such as \equiv.
Do not add any extra text before or after the JSON object.

Return exactly one JSON object in this format:
{
  "schema_name": "eywa_node_response",
  "schema_version": "v1",
  "orchestration_decision": "execute_locally",
  "decision_notes": "optional string",
  "response": "FINAL_ANSWER: <answer>\nJUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>",
  "result_type": "summary"
}

orchestration.decision_notes: The problem has been abstracted into a formal string theory and optimization problem involving prefix-free sets and lexicographical minimality.

Question:
Solve this Super-Eywa grading-bank question.
Question ID: coding-B1-usaco-2024-us-open-platinum-identity-theft
Title: B1. USACO 2024 US Open Platinum / Identity Theft

Problem:
Extend cows' bitstring IDs so no cow can be mistaken for another when some reported IDs are truncated.

Grading target:
Binary hidden-test acceptance.

Return format:
FINAL_ANSWER: <your answer>
JUSTIFICATION: <brief justification>

results.content:
The problem has been abstracted into a formal string theory and optimization problem involving prefix-free sets and lexicographical minimality.
node_root_helper_01 (transmuted_child): Given a set of N binary strings S = {s1, s2, ..., sN}, construct a set of extended binary strings E = {e1, e2, ..., eN} such that: 1. Extension Constraint: For every i in {1, ..., N}, si is a prefix of ei. 2. Prefix-Free Constraint: No string in E is a prefix of another string in E. That is, for all i != j, ei is not a prefix of ej. 3. Optimization Objective: Minimize the sequence of lengths (|e1|, |e2|, ..., |eN|) lexicographically. Note: The lexicographical minimization is applied to the vector of lengths. If multiple sets of lengths achieve the same minimum, any such set is valid, but the problem implies a unique minimal length vector for each si based on the construction requirements.
